nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, nutrient-rich, slow-moving or stagnant freshwater — ponds, swamps, rice paddies, and gently flowing streams across tropical and subtropical Asia

avoidWith: boisterous fish that uproot rosette plants

summary: Blyxa japonica is a bushy, grass-like rosette plant prized in Amano-style aquascapes. It generally does best with medium-to-high light, CO2, and iron-rich substrate, where it develops golden to reddish highlights.
careNotes: Blyxa japonica is a true aquatic rosette plant in the family Hydrocharitaceae, valued in iwagumi and nature aquariums for its soft, grass-like silhouette and the golden-to-reddish highlights it tends to develop under strong light with adequate iron. The trade name "Bamboo Plant" is sometimes used for it. It is generally considered an intermediate plant rather than beginner-friendly: sources vary from "easy" (Buce Plant) to "demanding and finicky" (Aquarium Breeder, 2HR Aquarist), with Tropica positioning it as a medium-demand plant. The averaged middle position is intermediate. Lighting is commonly described as medium to high; roughly 30–40 µmol PAR will keep it alive, ~100 µmol promotes a bronze hue, and ~200 µmol drives deeper reddening. CO2 is not strictly required but is strongly advised for compact, colorful growth (around 6–14 mg/L per Tropica, 20–30 mg/L per Aquarium Breeder). Substrate should be nutrient- and iron-rich (aquasoil or clay-based); it is a heavy root feeder and benefits from root tabs alongside moderate water-column fertilization. Temperature 23–26 °C, pH 6.0–7.5; pH spread across sources ran 5.5–7.5 and the central range was adopted. Plant by trimming the lowest leaves to expose a short stem and tucking individual rosettes into the substrate. Blyxa is sensitive to algae and disliked unstable water — keep parameters steady, avoid drastic changes (which can trigger melt and root rot), and maintain a clean tank. It is well-suited as a bushy midground accent and is often used in Amano-style aquascapes.
breedingNotes: Propagation is vegetative and straightforward in healthy tanks: mature plants produce lateral side shoots and small daughter plantlets at the base, which can be separated and replanted once they have their own roots. Long stems can also be trimmed and replanted directly into nutrient-rich substrate. Seed reproduction is documented in the wild but rarely used by aquarists.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Plant details page resolved from Tropica search for Blyxa japonica. Provided height (10–20+ cm), medium light demand (~0.5 W/L), medium CO2 (6–14 mg/L), medium growth rate, and Asian origin.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Hydrocharitaceae), height (4–8 in / ~10–20 cm), pH 6.5–7.5, midground/background placement, propagation by stem cuttings and runners, and noted that golden/red coloration develops under high light with CO2 and fertilization.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder
additional.notes: Used for native-range country list, taxonomy history, temperature (23–26 °C), pH 5.5–7.0, soft-water preference (gH 1–5), iron-rich substrate guidance, minimum tank size (~10 gal / 40 L), and the intermediate-to-difficult care assessment. pH range averaged with Buce Plant to 6.0–7.5.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy confirmation: family Hydrocharitaceae, order Alismatales; alternate names 'Japanese blyxa' and 'dwarf Asian grass'.
